data.title: Chevron: Buffett's Largest Energy Holding Looks Inexpensive
data.description:
The Q2 earnings season is over and the common theme that I recognized was that the AI story is far from over. The allocation toward CapEx continues to expand and more companies are looking for ways to cash in on the AI segment. I don’t believe that the technology segment is the only sector that will benefit from AI and that energy will be an indirect beneficiary from the increased demand. Chevron Corporation (NYSE:CVX) is Warren Buffett’s largest energy company as it represented 6.79% of Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s (BRK.A) (BRK.B) portfolio at the end of Q2 with Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY) following with a 4.81% allocation. CVX has a premier asset portfolio that is vertically integrated through its upstream, midstream, and downstream segments. I believe that CVX is undervalued as it is expected to expand its profitability due to the increasing demand for traditional energy sources. CVX has a large moat around its business, as its scale allows them to have low breakeven points per barrel. I believe that the current valuation is attractive as investors could experience significant appreciation in the share price while getting paid more than 4.25% from the annualized dividend in an interest rate environment that is declining.

Following up on my previous article about Chevron

In July of 2024, I wrote an article on CVX (can be read here) where I discussed why I felt shares of CVX were headed higher. The S&P has done exceptionally well since then as it has increased 19.01% while shares of CVX have only appreciated by 3.6%. When the dividend is accounted for CVX’s total return has been 9.51%. I felt that CVX offered a strong value opportunity as it was becoming abundantly clear that we needed more oil and gas not less of it. Companies were starting to ramp up their CapEx allocations toward data centers and U.S. oil production was starting to break out above 13 million bpd again. I am following up with a new article on CVX as I believe there is a lot of forward potential for this investment case. CVX has completed its acquisition of HESS, achieved record production levels, entered the U.S. lithium sector, and is trading at a low valuation. I think that shares of CVX will become more popular as the Fed lowers rates due to their dividend yield which exceeds 4.25%. Income investors are likely to move capital from the sidelines back into the capital markets as rates decline and the fact that CVX has increased its dividend for 37 years will provide

Chevron looks undervalued at the current valuation and I believe that the energy landscape is supporting a strong outlook

When I compare CVX to the other supermajors and some other dividend staples, including The Coca-Cola Company (KO), The Procter & Gamble Company (PG), and Walmart Inc. (WMT), I believe that CVX looks inexpensive. CVX is expected to generate $7.87 of EPS in 2025 putting its valuation at 20.18 times 2025 earnings. Over the next 2 years CVX is expected to grow its EPS to $11.68 which is an increase of 48.41%. CVX is trading at 13.6 times 2027 earnings which is a bit higher than Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) but it has significantly more growth on the horizon. I think that investors can get a lot of growth at a low valuation with shares of CVX and they are also getting rewarded through the robust dividend program. CVX is paying an annualized dividend of $6.84 which is a 4.27% yield based on the current share price. CVX has increased the dividend for the past 37 years, and the dividend growth rate is 5.96% over the past 5 years. Investors can lock in a dividend yield that is exceeding the 10-year with annualized dividend increases. I think there is a lot of value to be unlocked as shares of CVX could generate a lot of capital appreciation while providing significant amounts of dividend income.

When I look at the energy landscape it looks to support the analyst projections about CVX’s forward EPS. The U.S. is producing the most oil in its history at 13.6 million bpd and CVX is playing a significant role in this. According to the EIA the global production and consumption levels will continue to increase throughout the remainder of 2025 and through 2026. This is very bullish for CVX as there will be significant demand for barrels being produced from the new growth projects it brings online. These projections could also be underestimated depending on how much the allocation towards CapEx changes from the hyperscalers over the next several years.

When I look at the amount of capital being allocated toward CapEx from Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) it's not slowing down. The bullish aspect for CVX and the energy industry is that more energy is required during construction and post-construction as it takes a large amount of electricity to run a data center. The combination of their CapEx in Q2 2025 amounted to $88.25 billion. This was an increase of 66.97% ($35.39 billion) YoY. The growth rate that these companies are spending to build out data centers is expanding considering that in Q2 2024 the combination of these 4 companies increased their CapEx spend QoQ by 19.33% ($8.56 billion) to $52.85 billion. I think there are many energy companies that will benefit from the AI revolution and CVX is in a prime position to benefit from the data center expansion that is currently occurring.

Chevron delivered a strong Q2 and I believe it's positioned for growth

CVX delivered a record-breaking quarter for production which was supported by averaging more than 1 million bpd of oil equivalent in the Permian Basin. This showcased CVX’s ability to execute a strategic vision as they introduced the timeline to reaching 1 million BPD 5 years ago. CVX has been drilling in the Permian for over a century as they have more than 2 million net acres and significant mineral interests that span roughly 75% of its total acreage in the basin. CVX has almost doubled their oil production over the past 5 years while improving well designs, reducing cycle times, and developing new technology that has led to a 30% reduction in development and production unit costs. CVX’s global portfolio has demonstrated exceptional performance as their first growth project at the Tengiz (TCO) asset in Kazakhstan is now producing at full rates, and the facility is operating 18% above its original expected capacity. In the Gulf of America, production is ramping up from their recent major project start-ups, while improved recovery techniques on base assets have enhanced estimated ultimate recovery by 9%. CVX’s Australian LNG facilities are operating 7% above their original capacity as well. CVX’s downstream business also recognized its highest U.S. refinery crude throughput in over 20 years. CVX has made these achievements through its optimization efforts and top-quartile turnaround performance.

I believe that CVX has positioned itself for growth as it has undertaken significant strategic initiatives that are designed to enhance efficiency, reduce costs, and position the company for new avenues of growth. CVX has restructured its organization and reduced the number of upstream reporting units by approximately 70% by grouping together similar asset classes like the shale and tight businesses in the Permian, DJ, Bakken, and Argentina. This is intended to scale best practices faster, standardize solutions, and streamline support functions. CVX is expanding centralized engineering hubs and global business services to drive further standardization and value. These changes are expected to achieve $2 billion to $3 billion in structural cost reductions by the end of 2026. In Q2 CVX took its first step into the U.S. lithium sector as they acquired approximately 125,000 net acres of lithium-rich land in Texas and Arkansas. CVX is planning to establish a scalable domestic business in direct lithium extraction to further diversify its growth opportunities. CVX was able to increase its frontier acreage portfolio by over 20% in the last two years and secured new offshore exploration blocks in Brazil and Egypt and has wells planned for Suriname, Namibia, and Egypt by the end of 2025. CVX has also entered into long-term contracts in its liquefied natural gas ((LNG) segment, which will increase its total U.S. Gulf Coast offtake capacity to 7 million tonnes per year.

I believe that CVX’s forward growth will be expedited by the Hess acquisition as it will create a premier international energy company by adding world-class assets. Hess’s portfolio allowed CVX to expand its shale portfolio and the combined upstream portfolio is now projected to lead the industry in total cash generation through the remainder of the decade. This deal is expected to be cash flow accretive by Q4 2025 and CVX is accelerating the integration process and expects to realize the full $1 billion in annual run-rate synergies by the end of 2025 which is 6 months ahead of the original guidance. CVX’s total shale and tight oil production is expected to increase to 1.6 million BOE/D which represents 40% of their total output. CVX’s focus for its shale assets is shifting from growth to generating substantial FCF by holding production levels, which will support making investments throughout its entire portfolio. This will help CVX maintain its balance sheet and fund their capital allocation program back to shareholders. CVX is now projecting that through the integration of HESS they will be able to increase their FCF by $12.5 billion in 2026. I am exceptionally bullish on CVX because the increased FCF allows them to expand their growth initiatives when opportunities present themselves.

Conclusion

I think that CVX is an undervalued gem that isn’t getting enough attention because of the segment it operates in. If CVX were a technology company it would have a much larger multiple considering the analyst community is projecting that they will grow EPS by almost 50% over the next 2 years. CVX is positioned to thrive over the next several years as more data centers come online and the HESS acquisition expands their footprint. The Fed just started to cut rates again which could stimulate the economy and also drive energy utilization higher causing CVX’s earnings to expand. CME Group is expecting that we could see rates decline by another 125 bps by the end of next year which would make the dividend yield from CVX of 4.27% much more attractive because risk-free assets will continuously generate less income while CVX raises the dividend. Ultimately, I think CVX is undervalued and there is tremendous value for investors to unlock as shares could go higher through next year.

data.title: BNDI: The Higher Income Appears To Be Return Of Capital
data.description:
Understand the machine

To work out what a machine does - and how well it does it - it's necessary to grasp more than just a little bit of what the machine is doing internally. This is as true of financial instruments—economic - economic machines - as it is of anything else.

Thus to work out what's really happening - and whether we'd like it to happen to us - it's worth looking at the details of the economic machine we might be interested in.

Neos Enhanced Income Aggregate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:BNDI)

The essential argument here is to be long bonds and then juice the income by writing options on that long position. As a basic strategy, this works - or, at least, can work.

If there are going to be options, there need to be those who write options. That's fairly obvious. The natural writers of options are those who are natural longs of the item in question. They get to juice their returns at the risk of having to deliver on that options contract.

Now, I have said about ULTY that I think this can be taken too far. Deliberately buying a position in order to write options on it doesn't seem to me to work for logical reasons. Of course, I could be wrong.

On the other hand, a long bond fund is, well, it's a natural long. They're going to sit there for decades, and why not try to juice that return? As with closed end funds leveraging up, yes, of course there are risks, but the juiced returns might well make it worth it.

We can also go one more level of complication, which is to create, at low cost, that long bond position - say buy a lot of stock in ETFs, which are themselves long bond funds - and then write options using that as the natural long. As a logical position, seems fine to me.

Which is what BNDI does

BNDI does exactly this. Buys into two long bond funds and then writes put options on that position:

Or in slightly more detail:

I'm not wholly and exactly certain that a position in bonds is a natural long against S&P options, even as I am certain that those holdings in bond ETFs are just fine as security against S&P options.

But that's the strategy. The question is, how well does it work?

Slightly mixed evidence

So we've the claimed distribution rate or yield. Seeking Alpha has it at 5.63%. Neos shows it as higher:

The difference between x.63% and x.77% can be something as simple as which day of the month we take as our price reference - that reference price is obviously at its lowest the day it goes ex-div, and it rises by the amount of the dividend over the month.

If for simplicity's sake we just take the Seeking Alpha numbers for the underlying bond funds, they're paying 3.79% and 3.74% against the similarly calculated 5.63%. So, that options strategy is performing as it should. It's juicing the return on the underlying by those 2 percentage points (in bonds, that's a lot), and that's great.

But we also get slightly mixed evidence here. That distribution/share % is how much of the distribution is "return of capital" and how much is actual earnings? Just for ease of calculation, turn the 0.48% into a 0.5%, and we can now say that BNDI is yielding—as earnings—2.81%. Which is actually less than the underlying funds by near a full percentage point. The underperformance is actually greater than the 0.58% BNDI charges.

So we've a problem here

What actually is the return from BNDI?

Well, we've two separate claims about returns here:

And:

And that's all rather difficult, because:

Note that we've not got to worry about net asset value getting away from market price and so on. This is an ETF, so any price gaps that open up will be tenths of a percent maximum.

So we've got something where the market price is observably down since launch - 4% perhaps, just as a number between friends. Yet we've a claim of a market price up 11.72% since inception - within a couple of weeks of 3 years ago (launch was 30 Aug 2022).

4% down is different from 11.72% up. A 5.75% distribution rate - if we assume it's from earnings - is different from a 2.81% earnings yield plus what's made up to the 5.75% by return of capital.

So, what's going on here?

In this whole sector - juicing returns by writing options on long assets in the fund - we've a conceptual problem. Exactly how are we to describe those options premiums? They're not, not really, yield as with the earnings from a bond or underlying ETF. There's also the point that sometimes the ETF just gets to collect the option premiums, and at other times it actually has to pay out to settle them - no one at all manages to write options all the time that never suffer from prices moving against them.

So, we've - conceptually - a problem with what is income, what is capital? If we classify the options earnings as a capital gain - could be sensible, we're almost certainly going to treat losses on options at maturity as a capital loss, but then also pay them out as part of the distribution. Is that part of the earnings distribution of "return of capital"?

This gets further complicated by the tax treatment:

Options-based income ETFs from NEOS aim to harness the tax efficiency of return of capital distributions for investors without eroding their underlying principle. Each ETF seeks to fund a portion of its monthly income distributions from sold index option premium, which is converted to capital gains, and then paid out to investors on a monthly basis.

As an aside, a principle is a moral stance, a principal is an amount of capital. Just one of those typos that bugs me (and yes, I know I'm guilty of many a typo myself).

So that option income is converted into a capital gain within the fund. Which then gets counted as a return of capital when distributed. Or that's the conclusion I come to, at least.

But this still doesn't solve the problem

Even if this is true - it is true too - how can we have the claim of an 11.72% rise in market price since inception when the market price is actually down 4% since inception?

Ah, well - and this happens in other parts of this sector too - the idea is that, clearly, there's this confusion between capital income and simple earnings given the mix-and-match idea of being long an instrument and then writing options for the income/capital gain. So, the contention becomes that the only way to properly measure performance is to assume whole and total reinvestment of the distribution back into the investment at each payment date. Then look at that as a total return over time.

So what is being presented as a rise in market value is, in fact, the value with full reinvestment of the distribution. What is the value of your position having reinvested your dividends, not what is the market value of your original investment after distributions?

These are, equally clearly, rather different measurements.

We can also check this quickly and roughly. So, a 5.75% return over 3 years is 17.25%. Not that the distribution has been that all the time, but still, rough and ready. If we subtract the 4% fall in market price, 13.25%. Which is really rather close to that 11.72% claimed rise in market price over the three years. With further refinement, we can probably get those two to merge properly as well.

So the real return from BNDI

The real return is that we can gain 5.75% as a yield at the risk of capital loss, which, over the past three years, has been $2 a share, or about 4% over the three years. At other times that capital loss has been larger and at times smaller - there's risk here. Or, if we don't take the income, if we reinvest everything, then we could have that 11.72% rise in market value. But we're not getting both - we're not getting a 5.75% yield *plus* a capital gain of 11.72%.

We can even combine those two measurements, the 4% capital loss and the 5.75% distribution over the three years. 4 divided by 3 is 1.33, so knock 1.33 off the 5.75%, and we've got 4.42% as a real annual return on our initial $50 investment. Yes, very rough and ready but usefully illustrative all the time.

Which is an interesting result from diving into the details of how the machine is constructed, no?

Why I'm wrong

Well, these options writing funds might well have yet more levels of complexity underneath them, and I could certainly be wrong if they do. It's also obviously true that the returns here depend upon market prices over these past three years. A different set of prices would change the returns.

My opinion

Once we've cleared up how they're reporting returns and gains, we can see that a useful and proper estimate of the yield (after accounting for that capital loss) is 4.42%. Which, OK, has juiced the return from those underlying bond funds, which are running at 3.7% and so on. But the construction has juiced those returns by less than we might think from the headline.

At which point, back to my favourite truism about financial markets. We get paid to take risks in markets. The corollary of that is the grand instruction about all financial markets: never take a risk you're not being paid for.

Here we're taking a risk - those options contracts could, conceivably, go haywire. Our return looks to be 4.42%. Which is the same we can get in floating rate Treasuries - so near zero credit or interest rate risk - at WisdomTree Floating Rate Treasury Fund ETF (USFR).

I don't think that makes sense. So, no, BNDI doesn't work for me.

data.title: 2 Fat Yields For Steady Income In Retirement, Yields +7%
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

The Wu-Tang Crew once sang an entire song about a simple concept - the dominion of cash. C.R.E.A.M - Cash Rules Everything Around Me - was highly successful and popular as it highlighted a common mindset in America. More money meant more power.

I don't chase a dollar bill for the sake of it, itself. I pursue financial freedom and financial security via investing. This is part of the heartbeat of my Income Method.

I invest with the goal of capturing cash flows from all activity around me; I demand to be paid now, for my time and money. We recently discussed the idea of getting paid by millions of customers, filling their tanks with gasoline on a daily basis. I enjoy tapping into the cash flows of everyday transactions.

Trillions of dollars' worth of transactions take place on a daily basis, and there are millions of participants in these exchanges. I invest for dividend income, to tap into the transactions made for and by public corporations in the country. Dividends paid out to shareholders by U.S. corporations continue to hit new records. The S&P reported that U.S. common stock dividends increased by $7.4 billion in Q2 2025.

But not all companies pay dividends. The younger and more growth-focused companies, especially from the tech sector, prefer to preserve and reinvest their profits to fuel more growth in the future, and promise investors greater share price appreciation. We are going to be increasingly dependent on technology in the years ahead, and there are means to tap into the cash flows of these growth-focused sectors.

Let us now discuss two diversified funds to generate income today, from non-dividend-paying, growth-focused companies.

Pick #1: CCD – Yield 11.1%

Convertible bonds are a class of security that lowers borrowing costs compared to traditional bonds without immediate shareholder dilution. These are hybrid securities that may carry a set coupon like a bond, and typically include an embedded option for the holder to convert it into a predetermined number of the issuing company's common stock shares at a specific time.

Convertibles are reserved for institutional investors, and retail investors can access them through diversified funds, such as Calamos Dynamic Convertible and Income Fund (CCD). With $820 million in managed assets and a deeply diversified portfolio of 601 securities (85% of which are convertibles), CCD presents an income-focused approach to invest in convertibles. Source

After trading at a sizable premium to NAV for months, it now presents an attractive buy at a modest 3% premium. The CEF also offers a DRIP discount of up to 5%, allowing reinvested distributions to be priced at NAV with a maximum discount of 5% off the market price (depending on participation by your broker).

CCD has had good years and bad years, in terms of market pricing, and this is based on the popularity of convertible issuance, which is driven by equity valuations and overall market appetite around growth. Despite this, CCD has delivered steady monthly income since its inception in 2015, allowing investors to stay calm through the inevitable convertible issuance cycles.

CCD employs Return of Capital in its distributions from time to time. However, as seen from the NAV performance below, the fund is using ROC as a means to pass through non-taxable cash flows from its investments. About 15% of FY 2024 distributions were ROC, and 37% LTCG.

CCD operates with a 33% leverage to boost returns from active management of convertibles. The borrowings carry a floating-rate of SOFR + 0.65%, positioning the fund for reducing interest expenses from rate cuts of any magnitude. CCD distributes $0.195 per share monthly, translating to an 11.1% annualized yield. At current modest valuations and with favorable tailwinds for the convertible asset class, the fund presents an attractive income opportunity.

Pick #2: BST – Yield 7.4%

BlackRock Science and Technology Trust (BST) is a CEF (Closed-End Fund) focused on investing at least ~80% of its assets in companies within the technology sector. The fund's top holdings represent ~52% of invested assets, and include some of the biggest global tech corporations.

Despite the concentration in its top holdings, BST actually holds ~127 companies, and actively manages its portfolio to realize gains for shareholders.

Recently, BlackRock announced a change in the objectives and principal investment strategies of certain funds, including BST, in an effort to move away from the options writing strategy. BST's objective statement will read

"The Trust’s investment objectives are to provide total return and income primarily through long-term capital appreciation." - Press Release

We believe this move will be a positive for BST, to realize better capital appreciation and continue its outperformance against popular market indices.

BST pays monthly distributions of $0.25/month, calculating to a 7.4% annualized yield. ~99% of BST’s distributions in FY 2024 comprised long-term capital gains, making it highly advantageous for shareholders from a taxation standpoint.

BST does not utilize leverage in its investment strategy. The fund had its IPO in October 2014 at an NAV of $19.06/share. Over these ~11 years, the fund has distributed $26.71/share, and currently has an NAV of $43.57/share, clearly indicating that BST is earning its payouts.

BST trades at a 6% discount to NAV, making it a discounted means to access the richly valued tech sector and collect regular income in the process. You can reinvest or use the funds to fuel your lifestyle; the choice is yours, and does not rest with an executive.

Conclusion

Investors tend to feel very passionate about their picks, often arguing how their choice is the best and the only logical one in the sector. No matter what parameters you use to determine what is best, there are going to be times when something else (perhaps even from the same sector) will do better.

While many investors focus on the OR – choosing one over another – at High Dividend Opportunities, we use the AND mindset. We are always buyers, seeking to add to our income streams. We don't shy away from buying more than one security from a sector, and it doesn't have to be the best in that sector, according to a defined set of metrics that mean nothing to us.

We buy into every aspect of the markets to generate rich dividends to support a retirement lifestyle of our choosing. This is the power of our Income Method, and the flexibility of income investing.

Stop thinking of C.R.E.A.M. and embrace I.N.C.O.M.E. - Income Now Covers My Everything

data.title: Virtus SGA U.S. Large Cap Growth SMA Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • Returns in Q2 were 9.6% (Gross) and 8.8% (Net) compared to the Russell 1000 Growth Index return of 17.8% and the S&P 500 Index return of 10.9%.
  • Q2 2025 saw a dramatic reversal of the weaknesses in Q1 as early concerns of extensive tariffs turned to enthusiasm for trade deals and tariff pauses.
  • AI and technological advances excited investors once more, reasserting the performance pattern of recent years. The largest 10 companies which are heavily exposed to these areas outperformed the rest of the index by 7.1%.
  • The top 10 companies in the Russell 1000 Growth Index now represent over 60%, creating risks and opportunities.
  • SGA’s Information Technology sector weighting is 37%, compared to 51% in the Russell 1000 Growth Index, avoiding extreme concentration risks.
  • Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA) and Meta (META) were the top contributors in the quarter while UnitedHealth (UNH), Thermo Fisher (TMO) and Aon (AON) were the largest detractors.
  • We initiated new positions in Chipotle (CMG), Arm Holdings (ARM), and Cooper (COOP). Three positions were liquidated during the quarter – Starbucks (SBUX), Thermo Fisher, and Autodesk (ADSK). Early in July, Novo Nordisk was liquidated and a position in Nike (NKE) was established.
  • SGA sees continued opportunity in attractively priced, predictable, cash-generative and growing businesses, which may become more sought after if market volatility increases and growth decreases.

Investment Objective

SGA builds high-conviction portfolios focused on quality growth businesses that are anticipated to achieve consistent mid-teens earnings growth with reduced variability, supported by predictable revenue and cash flow generation. The firm's investment goal is to convert this stable portfolio earnings growth into a return profile designed to protect and reliably compound client wealth over time.

Revenue and EPS Growth

Source: SGA Earnings and Revenues Estimates and Adjustments, Bloomberg, Russell, and FactSet. SGA Revenue and Earnings Growth calculated as the weighted average of all securities held in the portfolio during each calendar year.

Performance

The U.S. Large Cap Growth portfolio generated 14% revenue growth and 24% earnings growth in 2024 and is forecast for 11% earnings growth in 2025. The portfolio is currently forecast to generate 13% revenue and 14% earnings growth over the next three years.

The U.S. Large Cap Growth portfolio has generated 11.0% (Gross) and 7.7% (Net) annualized returns since inception, compared to 12.7% and 10.9% for the Russell 1000 Growth Index and S&P 500 Index, respectively. Over the trailing one-year period, the SGA portfolio returned 12.5% (Gross) and 9.2% (Net) compared to 17.2% and 15.2% for the Russell 1000 Growth Index and S&P 500 Index respectively.

Q2 2025 U.S. Index Returns

Market leadership changed in Q1 and then reverted in Q2, with index concentration once again posing a headwind. Returns for the quarter were 9.6% (Gross) and 8.8% (Net) compared to the Russell 1000 Growth Index return of 17.8% and the S&P 500 Index return of 10.9%.

Market Context: Navigating Policy Shifts in the U.S. Market

The second quarter of 2025 presented a notable shift in U.S. equity market dynamics following a challenging Q1. The Russell 1000 Growth Index, which experienced a -10% return in Q1 2025, demonstrated a strong recovery in Q2. By the end of Q2 the recovery was such that both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite Index hit fresh all-time highs. This is particularly remarkable when noting that the S&P 500 began the quarter with a decline of over 10%, making this only the third time in history that the index has recovered from such a precipitous drop within the same quarter.1

In Q1 we highlighted the portfolio’s strong outperformance against the Russell 1000 Growth Index in a declining market due to prudent portfolio construction and valuation discipline, but the flipside of this coin is that the portfolio struggled to keep up with the renewed momentum from the top 10 companies and AI enthusiasm that drove markets in Q2. Year to date, that left the portfolio returns at 4.7% (gross) and 3.1% (net), while the Russell 1000 Growth Index was ahead at 6.1% and the S&P 500 Index was ahead at 6.2%, led by the largest index constituents. The difference between the Russell 1000 Growth and the Russell 1000 Value was a colossal 14% for the quarter.

Geopolitically, the quarter began with an uneasy truce in the escalating tariff standoff between the U.S. and its key trading partners. The Trump rhetoric of reshoring incentives and protective tariffs remains the backdrop, particularly targeting goods from China, Mexico, and Canada. The market began to look through the rhetoric as the U.S. started negotiating with trade partners and struck early bilateral trade deals with the UK and China. President Trump announced temporary pauses and rollbacks for some countries and products. Retaliatory measures from trading partners were more measured than anticipated, creating a more stable backdrop. It is also likely that the adverse bond market reaction undermined Trump’s ability to take a harsher line on trade and hastened a softening of tone. The U.S. dollar has weakened by around 7% against most major global currencies as the Fed paused interest rate reductions and concerns remained about the U.S. trade deficit.

The economic uncertainty of one quarter ago does not seem to have abated significantly but equity markets have risen near, or exceeded, highs seen following the U.S. presidential election. Businesses began adapting supply chains, but fears of a sudden collapse in trade volumes began to subside and investor confidence increased. In the U.S., inflation moderated after a surprise uptick in Q1 and is near the Federal Reserve’s 2% target, enabling the Fed to hold rates steady while maintaining a data-dependent stance. Real interest rates remained elevated, but expectations for late-2025 rate cuts began to firm as wage growth decelerated and inventory data softened.

Technology regained leadership after underperforming in Q1. The sector benefited from a more nuanced understanding of AI's commercial trajectory, and the sell-off in high-growth names reversed sharply as earnings and capex data validated long-term theses.

The top 10 companies in the Russell 1000 performed significantly better than those outside the top 10. The difference in the quarter was 7.1%, overshadowing most other factors in importance for analyzing performance relative to the Russell 1000 Growth Index. The top 10 companies account for over 60% of the Russell 1000 Growth Index by value.

Artificial Intelligence: A Foundational Investment Theme Across the Computing Stack

Our AI investments are concentrated in foundational infrastructure that enables AI advancement rather than pursuing individual algorithmic breakthroughs. This positioning reflects our belief that at this stage, sustainable value creation will accrue to companies providing essential infrastructure, possessing proprietary data assets, or maintaining positions across the computing stack.

Recent developments like DeepSeek's (DEEPSEEK) optimization techniques underscore that AI computing remains prohibitively expensive for broad adoption. We anticipate dramatic cost reductions over the next several years through hardware advances from companies like Nvidia and software optimization. This trajectory should unlock AI's commercial potential beyond current narrow applications.

Investing Across the Computing Stack; Not in LLMs

Our investment philosophy gravitates toward AI infrastructure and enterprise software beneficiaries rather than Large Language Models themselves, recognizing sustainable value accrues to businesses controlling essential resources and improving productivity. LLMs face rapid obsolescence and unclear monetization paths.

The portfolio's AI exposure spans the computational stack:

  • Design Tools: Synopsys (SNPS) and Arm Holdings (ARM) provide essential IP and design capabilities
  • Compute Platforms: Nvidia's data center business offers predictable revenue
  • Cloud Infrastructure: Microsoft, Amazon (AMZN), and Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) provide computational fabric
  • Enterprise Software: ServiceNow (NOW), Intuit (INTU), and Microsoft translate AI into business outcomes

This diversified approach allows growth projection across layers, building conviction through multiple confirmation points while managing risk through top-to-bottom stack exposure.

Market Dynamics and Competitive Landscape

AI development requires significant capital, data, computing power and power resources, favoring established businesses such as Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, Microsoft, and Nvidia, and creating barriers to early-stage companies which face challenges accessing quality data for model training. These barriers suggest size, scale, deep pockets, and innovation are essential, suiting leading, cash-generative growth companies.

Investment Rationale and Risk Management

Our AI investments are made looking through the same lens of our required investment characteristics: companies demonstrating revenue repeatability, pricing power translating to robust margins funding R&D, and cultures of continuous innovation expanding into growth opportunity. While confident in AI's transformative potential, we maintain disciplined position sizing based on three-to-five-year horizons, avoiding overconcentration while maintaining meaningful exposure.

Looking forward, we believe software will become increasingly sophisticated with intelligent agents performing complex tasks autonomously. The convergence of cheaper compute and improved algorithms should accelerate adoption in robotics and humanoid applications.

The key challenge involves translating AI capabilities into substantial revenue generation beyond cost reduction. Companies showing clear paths to revenue enhancement through AI integration will likely command premium valuations and deliver superior returns.

Health Care Update

As the chart beside shows, the Health Care sector’s relative performance over the last five years is near the bottom percentile of data since 1972. In this difficult backdrop, we have also had company specific challenges, which has resulted in Health Care impacting our relative performance significantly.

Specifically, we have seen policies in the U.S. such as the IRA drug price negotiation and the threat of pharmaceutical tariffs or further drug price cuts impact companies that cater to the biopharma industry such as Icon (ICLR) and Danaher (DHR). UnitedHealth (UNH) has also experienced challenges from changes to reimbursement rates from U.S. regulators, and Novo Nordisk has had pipeline disappointments as well as challenges posed by lack of regulatory action against copycatting of its drugs by thousands of smaller direct to consumer outlets.

Recognizing that some of these policies have had a greater impact on near-term earnings, we have re-examined our theses and focused client capital where our long-term conviction remains in both the growth potential and growth resiliency.

Largest Contributors

Microsoft

Microsoft has successfully leveraged its portfolio of products into a comprehensive platform centered on an intelligent cloud and intelligent edge, effective in addressing customer needs. The company’s products are sticky with a high cost of switching for customers, providing attractive pricing power. Microsoft’s recurring revenues continue to grow as more products are incorporated into the cloud and edge architecture. Microsoft was a top contributor to performance during the quarter, driven by a strong quarterly report with accelerating growth in its cloud business and continued momentum in AI adoption. Cloud growth accelerated to 35%, almost a 5% beat versus expectations driven by a faster recovery in non-AI workloads and sustained demand for AI infrastructure. Management guided to similar growth for the June quarter, suggesting this momentum may be sustainable over the second half of the year, and in our view, longer. Microsoft’s broad product portfolio, high recurring revenue base, and strong positioning in both productivity software and cloud infrastructure continue to provide resilience and long-term growth potential. We maintained an above-average weight position in the company.

Nvidia

Nvidia is a leader in design of computer graphics processors (GPUs), system on chips, and related software. GPUs can process many pieces of data simultaneously, making them useful for a myriad of computationally intensive applications. An increasing number of industries are leveraging their technology to automate, enhance, and accelerate their own businesses by incorporating agentic AI, accelerated computing, and recommendation engines. The pace of innovation is extremely rapid in Nvidia’s markets. Nvidia’s shares were strong during the quarter as the company continued to benefit from surging demand for accelerated computing and AI infrastructure. Data center revenue remained the dominant driver of the company’s results, supported by strong growth in inferencing workloads and increasing adoption of generative AI across enterprise and consumer applications. Given concerns around export restrictions to China, CEO Jensen Huang might launch a China-specific product and establish local facilities to offset geopolitical risks. We are less optimistic on sustained momentum there unless there is a long-term geopolitical resolution. The company’s leadership in high-performance GPUs, combined with its full- stack software ecosystem and growing exposure to recurring revenue streams, increase our confidence in its growth trajectory. As a key enabler of end markets like AI and autonomous driving, we believe that the business can continue to grow from here over the next decade and the long-term opportunity remains significant as AI workloads scale and new use cases emerge. We maintained an above-average weight position, trimming on strength during the quarter.

Meta

Meta was a contributor to performance, supported by strong first quarter results with sales up 19% on a constant currency basis and operating profit up 27%. Management guided second quarter sales up 12% on a constant currency basis, ahead of analyst expectations. In the second quarter, the company is aggressively ramping up AI investments, including a $14 billion investment in Scale AI, and the formation of its “super-intelligence” team, led by Scale AI’s CEO, Alexandr Wang. Despite regulatory headwinds especially in the EU, Meta continues to see tangible AI-driven gains in ad pricing and engagement across platforms. Meta’s leading position amongst social media networks gives the company critical leverage with advertisers, users, and even creators. While near-term investments may pressure margins and cash flow, we believe Meta is well- positioned to unlock meaningful long-term growth. Its scale, first-party data, and platform integration across Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp provide a durable competitive advantage. We continue to view Meta as a high-quality compounder with attractive long-term growth potential and maintained an above-average weight in the stock.

Largest Detractors

UnitedHealth

UnitedHealth (UNH) is the largest health insurance and health services organization in the U.S. and operates two different businesses, UnitedHealthcare, its medical insurance business, and Optum Health, its service business delivering low-cost, high-quality care. A series of disappointing developments raised questions about the company’s near-term earnings power and execution. The company revised its 2025 guidance downward due to significantly higher-than-expected care activity in its Medicare Advantage (MA) population and a worse-than-anticipated risk profile among newly acquired members. These pressures were most acute in the Optum Health segment, which operates under capitated value-based care arrangements and was disproportionately impacted by the ongoing phase-in of the CMS (Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services) v28 risk adjustment model. This is a set of rules used to predict healthcare costs for Medicare beneficiaries which will be fully implemented in 2025. The rule change has created near-term pressure on UNH’s margins. While management attributed the earnings shortfall to transitory factors and maintained that the issues are addressable, the magnitude of the miss and the suspension of full-year guidance undermined confidence in the company’s ability to navigate a more challenging regulatory and utilization environment. The abrupt CEO transition added to investor uncertainty, though we view the return of former CEO Stephen Hemsley as a potentially stabilizing force. Hemsley brings deep institutional knowledge and a track record of disciplined execution, and his reappointment may help accelerate operational improvements and restore investor confidence. Separately, reports of a potential DOJ criminal investigation into Medicare Advantage billing practices have surfaced, adding to the headline risk. While the details remain unclear, a recent Special Master recommendation in a related civil case favored UnitedHealth, which may help mitigate some of the near-term overhang. We continue to believe in the long-term opportunity of UnitedHealth’s integrated care model and its leadership position in Medicare Advantage and value-based care.

We have tempered our near-term assumptions to reflect recent execution missteps and external pressures, and under conservative scenarios, we see a compelling investment opportunity. The company’s pricing power is enabled by the scale and data provided through its integrated model, which positions UNH to help its customers lower costs while managing its own profitability. Most of the healthcare insurance and related services are non-discretionary, and a large portion of UNH’s revenues are derived from highly recurring client contracts. As a leader in managed care, we believe the company is exceedingly well-positioned to help address the critical issue of rising healthcare costs in the U.S. and benefit from the continued privatization of government run programs through Medicare Advantage. We lowered the target to a below- average weight position following the stock’s weakness in the quarter, however, we purchased additional shares considering our view of the thesis moving forward.

Thermo Fisher

Thermo Fisher shares lagged during the quarter, and we exited the position due to forced attrition given increased uncertainty about biopharma industry R&D budgets and a lower growth outlook. While the company remains well managed and continues to benefit from its scale and breadth of offerings, the external environment has shifted meaningfully. Publicly funded research, academic science, and biotech R&D, all key end-markets for Thermo Fisher, remain under pressure, particularly in the U.S. and China. Although management has taken steps to reposition the portfolio, including investments in U.S.-based capacity and acquisitions in areas like CRO services, we believe these moves are unlikely to fully offset the headwinds from declining Covid-related revenues and a more constrained funding backdrop. Additionally, we have concerns that recent capital deployment decisions may reduce the company’s exposure to higher-growth segments. While the company’s revised growth outlook reflects some of these realities, we believe it remains optimistic and does not fully account for the secular pressures facing its core markets. As such, we chose to reallocate the capital to other opportunities with higher secular conviction and margin visibility.

Aon

Aon is a leading professional services firm that advises companies on how best to manage risk, healthcare benefits, and retirement obligations. The advice is monetized through recurring commissions, fees and subscriptions that are annually renewing and sticky. Its customer base includes small, mid, and large global companies across 120 countries and many different industries. The percentage of industry premiums that brokers such as Aon take has been steady over time at around 10%. Building on its scale advantage, Aon is investing in data and analytics businesses to increase its market share and pricing power further. We believe that Aon can continue to grow reliably as it capitalizes its data and analytics capabilities. Aon’s first quarter results missed expectations, with organic revenue growth of 5%+ versus consensus of 6%+. A softening insurance cycle will be weighing on growth for insurance brokers such as Aon. However, we believe Aon is well-positioned relative to peers to navigate the evolving pricing environment and deliver resilient revenue growth. This is due to their higher exposure to fee revenues, as well as market share gains and general client behavior to buy more insurance when prices are lower. At Aon’s Investor Day in June, management reaffirmed guidance across financial metrics, including mid-single-digit or greater revenue growth, margin expansion, and double-digit free cash flow growth. With its strategic focus on driving both organic growth and operational efficiency, Aon is well placed to deliver on expected double-digit earnings growth over the next several years. We added to the position on weakness during the quarter maintaining an average weight.

Portfolio Activity

We initiated new positions in Chipotle, Arm Holdings, and Cooper. Three positions were liquidated during the quarter – Starbucks, Thermo Fisher, and Autodesk. Positions in Aon, Gartner (IT), Waste Management (WM), and Salesforce (CRM) among others were added to on weakness while Visa (V), ServiceNow, and Nvidia among others were trimmed on strength. Purchases

Chipotle

We initiated a new position in Chipotle and built it to an average weight during the quarter. Chipotle operates over 3,700 fast-casual restaurants, primarily in the U.S., offering a focused, customizable menu built around fresh, high-quality ingredients. The company has demonstrated strong pricing power, having successfully passed through multiple rounds of price increases with minimal impact on traffic. Chipotle maintains higher margins despite pricing 10–30% below many fast- casual peers, reinforcing its strong value proposition. Repeatable revenue is supported by its value proposition driving the habitual nature of these purchases and a rapidly growing loyalty program, which now exceeds 40 million members. This platform enables personalized marketing and drives higher frequency and ticket size, contributing to more predictable transaction volumes. Chipotle’s customer base skews toward higher-income consumers, which has helped the brand maintain resilient demand across economic cycles.

Management targets low-teens revenue and mid-teens profit growth, driven by 8–10% annual unit expansion, low to mid- single-digit same-store sales growth, and margin expansion. The company sees a long runway for U.S. growth with a target of 7,000 stores, nearly double its current footprint, and is beginning to scale internationally, with early traction in Canada and Europe. While international margins are currently lower, the domestic playbook is showing signs of success abroad. Chipotle’s unit economics remain compelling, with year-one cash-on-cash returns of ~40% and 60–70% at maturity, supporting continued expansion at attractive returns.

Risks we are monitoring include elevated labor turnover, which could impact store-level productivity and management depth, and the company’s ability to scale its concept internationally, where brand familiarity and unit economics are less proven. Additionally, while food safety practices have improved significantly since prior incidents, maintaining consumer trust remains essential to long-term brand equity. We believe Chipotle’s combination of pricing power, recurring customer engagement, and scalable growth opportunity positions it well to deliver consistent double-digit earnings and revenue growth over the coming years.

Arm Holdings

A new position in Arm Holdings, a leader in the design and licensing of central processing units (CPUs) and related sub systems, was initiated during the quarter. This is a company we owned in client portfolios in the past, having sold it most recently in 2016 when it was acquired by Softbank. Under Softbank, Arm significantly expanded its R&D and broadened its capabilities into full system designs and AI, diversifying beyond mobile processing and into servers, auto, and other consumer end markets. These efforts proved to be critical in positioning Arm and its architecture as the leading CPU design beyond mobile processing applications. Arm has established itself as a market leader in the CPU ecosystem as trends such as rising demand for battery-powered devices, optimizing power efficiency, and custom full stack optimized designs continue to strengthen its competitive position.

Arm is able to sustain its pricing power as switching out of a CPU design is very difficult due to backward compatibility and software ecosystem requirements. The company operates through a two-part revenue model consisting of licensing revenue and royalty revenue based on the value of chips sold. Arm’s licenses are typically annual fixed recurring subscriptions, providing predictable revenue streams which are supported by a retention rate in the high 90s. The company’s royalty revenue is tied to products sold and typically has long useful life, evidenced by 50% of Arm’s royalty revenue deriving from products launched over 10 years ago. Arm’s royalty rate is expected to grow significantly as the company extends its scope of design and captures more value of the ecosystem. Arm is well-positioned to benefit from secular trends for increasing AI workloads and demand for more powerful mobile computers that require low or battery power, including phone, electric vehicle, or other computers on the “edge” yet to come, further supporting a long runway for growth.

Among the key risks we are focused on today is the potential adverse impact of U.S. tariffs on sales of smartphone and other consumer electronics in the U.S., which will ultimately impact Arm’s royalty revenue growth. Another risk we are monitoring is the company’s exposure to China, which accounts for over 20% of revenue, given China’s strong desire to be self-sufficient in technology. Revenue from China is currently growing with strong customer demand, and the Chinese processor market is standardized on Arm. Arm is not directly subject to the U.S. export controls since most of its IP sits outside of the U.S. We are also monitoring potential competition from x86 and the open-source RISC-V architecture, which could present a risk for the market positioning of Arm, the pace of ARMv9 adoption, the latest iteration of the company’s processor architecture, and the impact of semiconductor cyclicality on royalty revenue growth rate.

Cooper

We initiated a new position in Cooper during the quarter. Cooper operates through two business units: CooperVision, which develops and manufactures a broad range of contact lenses, and CooperSurgical, which provides medical devices and services across women’s health and fertility industries. The company benefits from high barriers to entry in both segments due to regulatory and manufacturing complexity, and its broad product portfolio enables it to serve a wide range of clinical needs. In vision, Cooper’s extensive lens portfolio allows practitioners to fit nearly all patients, supporting strong share gains via new fits and trade-ups. Approximately one-third of CooperVision revenue comes from long-term private label contracts, which provide visibility to future cash flows.

The contact lens business, which accounts for two-thirds of revenue, is highly recurring, with patients rarely switching brands once fitted. CooperSurgical also benefits from long-term contracts and practitioner preference for specific devices, particularly in fertility, which is the faster-growing portion of the segment.

Management expects mid-single-digit market growth in vision and high-single-digit growth in fertility, with Cooper positioned to grow ahead of the market in both areas. The company is nearing the end of a multi-year capital investment cycle to expand its daily lens manufacturing capacity, which should support margin expansion and improved free cash flow conversion going forward.

Risks we are monitoring include incremental competition in the private label channel, currency volatility due to geographic revenue mix, and macro-related slowdowns in fertility treatment demand. Nonetheless, we view Cooper’s differentiated portfolio, strong customer relationships, and improving capital efficiency as key enablers of durable growth. We believe Cooper’s combination of recurring revenue, pricing power in lens categories, and exposure to secular growth drivers in both vision and fertility supports long-term low-double-digit earnings growth.

Post-Quarter End Portfolio Activity

Following the end of the quarter, we liquidated our position in Novo Nordisk and initiated a position in Nike. We sold Novo Nordisk out of the portfolio due to two concerns that made us question the company’s ability to deliver long-term growth.

Firstly, copycatting of Novo’s drugs by direct-to-consumer retail pharmacies is continuing beyond the May 22nd deadline. As background, due to a shortage earlier in 2024 and 2025, the FDA allowed legal copying of Novo’s weight loss drugs (“compounding”) to meet patient demand, with May 22nd marking the end of the authorization as the branded companies have restored sufficient supply. However, the compounders are using a loophole to continue marketing “personalized GLP-1 drugs”, which violates the spirit of the law. One of the retail pharmacies, Empower Pharmacy, is even challenging Novo competitor Eli Lilly’s composition of matter patent on its tirzepatide molecule with the PTAB (Patent Trial and Appeal Board) in an aggressive move. While there are a lot of legal actions to try to rein in the compounding pharmacies by both Novo and Lilly, it appears that this problem of intellectual theft is not likely to go away.

Secondly, at a recent diabetes meeting (American Diabetes Association, June 2025), incremental data regarding Novo’s Phase 3 trials of CagriSema suggested a negative risk/benefit profile. We had previously thought that despite disappointments in the trial data for CagriSema (such as weight loss not meeting the 25% goal), the drug was still safe with a higher efficacy than Novo’s currently marketed drugs. However, new data suggests potential safety concerns. The approval of CagriSema is important to Novo Nordisk, as the company needs to transition its diabetes and obesity franchise away from the semaglutide molecule before patient expiration in major markets in the early 2030s.

Specifically, there were two incidents of suicide out of total 3,008 patients in CagriSema’s Phase 3 trials. Due to the small number of events, it is not clear whether this consists of a true safety signal, and a third-party adjudication committee concluded that the events were not related to the drug. However, the rate of suicide is almost 5x the rate expected from this population in the Phase 3 trials so far. From our experience evaluating relevant precedents, the threshold for a clean approval by the FDA is very high based on the agency’s risk/benefit analysis framework applied to instances where safe, effective alternatives exist. There are many more CagriSema trials that will be reading out before Novo submits CagriSema for approval in early 2026, however we believe there is a new risk that has arisen in the approval pathway for CagriSema.

CagriSema combines cagrilintide (an amylin agonist) with semaglutide (a GLP-1 agonist). As semaglutide is a proven safe molecule (Ozempic and Wegovy), we asked the question whether cagrilintide is potentially causing a safety issue. Review of scientific literature suggests that there is a theoretical basis for the concern. We also noted that there was an increased incidence of depressed mood in a small trial for Lilly’s amylin molecule, eloralintide. Novo Nordisk’s pipeline is broader than CagriSema, however we note that the later stage products, such as amycretin, cagrilintide, and even the earlier stage triple agonist molecule, all share an amylin component, which we are worried could be causing a safety issue. We admit that there is no clear evidence at this point to say that there is a true safety risk. However, putting the puzzle together, we have become aware of a risk - which is broader than one molecule - that could be underappreciated in the pipeline at Novo Nordisk. Together with the continued concerns of compounding, which is a blatant violation of intellectual property and proven to be more difficult to rein in, we have decided to exit the position and reallocate the capital to iconic sportswear brand Nike.

Nike is a leading sportswear brand known for combining performance, utility, and durability with innovation, appealing to both sports and casual wear markets. Business growth is supported by strong marketing, supply chain management, and increasing adoption of athleisure lifestyles globally. Nike's brand reputation, technology, and robust supply chain, allow it to maintain margins better than competitors. Repeat purchases are driven by product wear and customer loyalty, especially in footwear, which constitutes the majority of sales and tends to retain customers due to comfort and performance benefits. Growth is fueled by rising sports participation, especially among women, e-commerce expansion, and emerging market penetration.

We had originally purchased Nike in U.S. Large Cap Growth portfolios in 2015 and held the position for nearly six years. Late in 2021, we liquidated the position due to a heightened valuation and short-term concerns around manufacturing and supply chain issues, as well as a volatile operating environment in China.

The company is embarking upon a change in strategic direction following a recent CEO transition to Elliott Hill, a 30+ year Nike veteran who had extensive experience across the organization before retiring from the company in early 2020 following the appointment of the company’s previous CEO, longtime technology executive John Donahoe. Under the previous leadership, Nike pursued a significant shift in sales strategy to focus on direct-to-consumer distribution through its own stores, website, and apps, to increase profit margins and gain more control over the customer experience. This focus on the direct channel coincided with a de-emphasis of the wholesale distribution channel, which allowed competitors to capitalize on Nike’s reduced presence to gain shelf space and take market share. At the same time, Nike over-distributed three of its classic footwear franchises (Air Force 1, Air Jordan 1, Dunk) and simultaneously fell behind on product innovation following an organizational restructuring that resulted in a loss of talent and focus.

Now ~8 months into his tenure as CEO, we believe Elliott Hill has taken the correct steps to reestablish the culture and approach that made Nike successful in the past, including leading with sport (instead of demographics), and re-focusing on innovation and broad distribution that touches a wide range of consumers. The company has rehired significant product/innovation talent, begun to reengage with previously abandoned wholesale distribution accounts, and is in the midst of managing down its classic franchises (to be essentially complete within the next six months) and opening up room in the marketplace for a strong flow of new innovative products in the coming quarters.

The company’s most recent financial results were weak overall but in line with expectations. We believe the business has bottomed out, with improvements expected each quarter going forward. There were some early positive indications on product innovation and an improving order book. Tariffs pose an additional near-term margin challenge, but the company has strategies in place that will fully mitigate their impact over time.

We have great confidence in Nike’s new leadership and expect they will succeed in the revitalization of an iconic business, delivering a sharp rebound in earnings growth in 2026 and 2027 following this reset in 2025.

Outlook

Broader markets experienced volatility once again due to geopolitical and macroeconomic developments. Global markets recovered, led by technology once more, as nerves calmed on President Trump's tariffs. Returns outside the U.S. were generally stronger than returns for the U.S. market and the weak dollar has clouded investor enthusiasm for the U.S. Despite the uncertainty, quality growth businesses in SGA’s portfolio, like Microsoft, Nvidia, and Meta performed well. Consistent with our investment philosophy, we took advantage of opportunities generated by market volatility to upgrade the portfolio including initiating new positions in attractively valued companies such as Chipotle, Arm Holdings, and Cooper. UnitedHealth, Thermo Fisher, and Aon disappointed over the quarter. We used the weakness to add to Aon and UnitedHealth, among others, while moving on from Thermo Fisher.

Headwinds from stubborn U.S. inflation, deglobalization, high deficits, and high debt levels continue to persist. Ongoing geopolitical tension and conflicts are likely to add to volatility. In a world of broadly slowing growth, we believe our portfolio of quality growth compounders with strong balance sheets, pricing power, and predictability, offers significant value relative to elevated stock market valuations.
