data.title: Essent Group: Upgrading To Hold As Interest Rates Decline
data.description:
In April, shortly after the market declines from “Liberation Day”, I wrote an analysis for Essent Group (NYSE:ESNT). The company is one of five major publicly traded companies that sell private mortgage insurance, or PMI. In my article, I rated the stock as a Sell due to the company’s slowing growth, declines in new PMI policies written, and increasing risk for loan defaults in the year ahead. My recommendation was not to actually short the stock, but rather to simply move out of ESNT and into other investments.

Alas, my recommendation has largely missed the mark. While I was correct that ESNT would trail the overall market, it has fared better than expected when compared to at least some of its peers. Since that article was published, ESNT has gained almost 14%, trailing the S&P 500 by just over 8% and lagging fellow PMI seller MGIC Investment Corporation (MTG) by about 1.5%. However, Essent Group has outperformed two other PMI providers, NMI Holdings (NMIH) and Enact Holdings (ACT), by about 1.5% and 8% respectively.

Out of all the companies in this industry, I still believe that Essent Group is one of the least attractive from a valuation standpoint. However, a couple of things have changed since my original analysis. The company has been able to beat profit expectations thanks to an active share repurchase plan. The housing market has also improved, albeit only slightly, thanks to 30-year mortgage rates that are now at their lowest point in almost a year. I am upgrading the stock to a Hold as I think there are a few reasons for optimism, but I would still not recommend adding to any positions in Essent Group at this time.

Company Overview

Founded in 2008 and based in Bermuda, Essent Group is a holding company with three subsidiaries as listed below. For a more detailed description of Essent and the nature of private mortgage insurance, readers are encouraged to look back at my first analysis for the company.

  • Essent Guaranty is a provider of private mortgage insurance for single-family homes.
  • Essent Reinsurance is a specialty reinsurer that safeguards a portion of Essent Guaranty’s PMI policies as well as some additional mortgages through Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.
  • Essent Title Insurance provides title insurance to home buyers in the US.

Essent Group has a market cap of $6.26 billion, making it the second largest of the five companies that deal exclusively in selling private mortgage insurance. Radian Group (RDN), the last of the group of five that I did not mention in my introduction, has recently announced its plan to diversify out of a stand-alone PMI provider model with the acquisition of Inigo. Even though ESNT has the second highest market value, the company has only the fourth largest PMI portfolio. At the end of Q2 2025, Essent’s mortgage insurance in force totaled $247 billion, which represented only a 3% increase from the same time in 2024. At this same time, Essent Re had risk in force of $2.3 billion.

ESNT currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.31 per share. This gives the stock a forward yield of 1.95%. The company last increased its payout by $0.03 per share at the start of this year. When compared to the other PMI providers, Essent Group has the lowest dividend yield in the group with the exception of NMIH, which does not currently pay a dividend.

Analysis of Essent Group

As part of its most recent quarterly report, Essent Group announced a Q2 profit of $1.93 per share. This was 12.9%, or $0.22, better than expected. Quarterly revenue of $319.1 million topped estimates by almost $900,000. As mentioned previously, one of the struggles for Essent has been its inability to show any signs of meaningful earnings growth. Q2 2025 earnings per share were only $0.02 ahead of the same period last year. Q1 2025 earnings were actually a penny less than Q1 2024.

The valuation for ESNT appears attractive on the surface. The stock trades at a forward PE for 2025 of 8.89. However, the company earned $6.84 per share in 2024. This year, expectations call for FY 2025 profits of $7.15 per share, showing an earnings growth rate of just 4.5%. Even looking ahead to next year, analysts are calling for FY 2026 earnings of $7.36 per share, which would reveal an even more tepid growth rate of 2.9%. When this information is factored in, ESNT appears to be teetering on the edge of being a value trap.

It should be noted that Essent Group is not trading at a bargain valuation on a historical basis. The graph below shows the PE ratio for its stock over the last decade. ESNT’s current trailing PE of 9.25 is the highest it has been in over four years. If the irregularity of the pandemic is taken out of consideration, then ESNT is as expensive today as it has been since early 2018.

There were some small improvements in the financial picture for Essent Group that could be seen in its most recent quarter of operations. The default rate for ESNT stands at 2.12%. Q2 was the second consecutive quarter of declines in that metric. At the end of Q4 2024, the number was 2.27%, an increase of 56 basis points from Q2 2024.

Perhaps one of the most impressive statistics for Essent Group has been its increase in book value per share since the end of FY 2013. From December 31, 2013, through the end of Q2 2025, the book value for ENST has grown at an annualized rate of just over 18%. The stock trades at a price to book ratio of just 1.15 when the stock’s current share price is used. However, once again, valuations for Essent Group and the other players in this space can be somewhat deceiving. The value here is not clear cut. The price to book ratio for ESNT is still higher than the same metric for Enact Holdings and Radian Group which trade at a level below 1.1.

Essent Group is supporting its stock with high volumes of share repurchases. From January 1, 2025 through June 30, 2025, the company bought back over 5.75 million shares of its common stock at a cost of approximately $328.5 million. The company still has over $150 million remaining on its most recent authorization that was approved in February of this year.

Analysis of PMI Market

Many of the concerns about the business operations for Essent Group could be alleviated if the housing market in the US was in better shape. Higher interest rates over the last couple of years has hampered home-buying activity. However, there is hope that at least some relief could be on the way.

The average rate for a 30-year fixed mortgage in the US dropped to 6.35% this week as the markets began to fully accept the likelihood of the Federal Reserve’s 25 basis point cut that was announced on Wednesday. Interest rates have been stubbornly moving in a relatively narrow range between 6.5% and 7% for most of 2025. As rates drop, affordability for homes increases which could bring some home buyers back to a real estate market that has been suppressed for over a year.

A recent report from CNBC showed just how impactful even a small improvement in interest rates can have on mortgage refinance activity. With mortgage rates dropping to their lowest rates in almost a year, applications for refinancing rose 58% last week when compared to the previous week. Applications increased an even more impressive 70% when juxtaposed against the same week from 2024. As rates fall, refinancing is taking a larger share of total mortgage activity, but this by itself, is not necessarily a good thing for Essent and the other PMI providers.

While any one company may be able to capture new business when a home loan is refinanced, there is also one company that is losing that policy when the first loan is paid off. There is no evidence to show that one PMI provider has a significant distinct advantage here. The net effect is a zero-sum game where one company wins when another loses. What Essent needs is new policies to be written on new home sales as overall home buying activity increases. That has not happened yet, but lower rates make it more likely in the future.

Added Risks to Consider

There is no guarantee that mortgage rates will continue to fall just because the Federal Reserve is lowering rates. The actions by the Fed set short-term rates. Fixed mortgage rates are long-term rates. Adjustable mortgage rates are more likely to be impacted by the Fed’s cuts in 2025. If long-term rates stay elevated, new home sales will stay sluggish, and opportunities for Essent to expand its share in the market will be limited.

Mortgage default rates tend to rise as unemployment rates rise. The Bureau of Labor and Statistics said that the unemployment rate in August was 4.3%. In June of this year, the number was 4.1%. Rising unemployment was the main reason that the Fed decided to drop its Fed funds rate by a quarter point at its September meeting. By historical standards, unemployment in the US is still relatively low, but the trend is moving in a direction that may cause headwinds for the PMI industry down the road.

In my first analysis of the company, I noted that insider trading activity for Essent Group was all negative in 2024. Since June 16 of this year, insiders have continued to unload stock with no purchases to report. A total of five selling transactions have been recorded from three different executives for a total of almost $1 million in shares sold.

The Bottom Line

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating appears to agree with my current assessment. The Hold rating is supported by grades of “A+” in Profitability and Revisions. The high mark for revisions is somewhat misleading, however, as the company has six upward revisions for earnings projections over the last three months but four downward revisions for revenue.

From my perspective, the worst may be over for Essent Group, but the company and the overall industry in which it operates is not completely out of the woods. Valuations look tempting on the surface, but this is an industry where single-digit PE ratios are the norm. I need to see real signs that buyers are returning to the market in larger numbers before I would consider ESNT to be a Buy.

data.title: Why Retirees Don't Need A Bond Allocation
data.description:
One of my most controversial opinions as a financial analyst is that retirees do not necessarily need bonds.

It has become the norm for investors to gradually increase their allocations to bonds as they age and approach retirement to lower their risk and earn higher income.

It can certainly make sense for some investors. If you have a low tolerance for risk, cannot handle volatility, and just want a secure income to get by, then a large bond allocation can make sense.

But for most investors, I think that it is a big mistake to allocate heavily into bonds, as you are essentially trading long-term inflation protection for lower near-term volatility, a tradeoff that doesn’t make sense for most of us in my opinion.

It is true that bonds do a good job at stabilizing a portfolio, but the opportunity cost is steep, and even retirees commonly still have 20+ years ahead of them, making inflation protection much more important than portfolio stabilization.

Let’s illustrate this with an example:

Today, 30-year Treasuries (US30Y) are yielding right around 4.6%. Assuming that your tax rate is 30%, you are left with a 3.2% yield after taxes. Then we still need to account for inflation. The 20-year expected average annual inflation rate is 2.4%. Therefore, your real after-tax return is just 0.8% per year. If you spend more than, your capital will lose in purchasing power over time.

And that’s assuming that everything works out well over the next 20 years and we avoid a major acceleration in inflation or a monetary collapse, which could cause your bonds to become worthless.

This may seem highly unlikely to some of you, but remember that historically, most fiat currencies have eventually collapsed or lost most of their value. Moreover, our governments are today more indebted than ever before, and the deficit is through the roof.

My point is that the risk of a major monetary black swan is not 0, and in that scenario, bonds and Treasuries could lose big.

Is earning a 0.8% after-tax real return worth this risk just for the sake of portfolio stabilization?

I don’t think so.

I would much rather accept higher volatility and invest in other income-generating investments that offer better long-term inflation protection.

That’s what we are doing at High Yield Investor.

We invest heavily in defensive sub-segments of the equity market that generate inflation-protected income. Good examples are MLPs (AMLP), BDCs (BIZD), REITs (VNQ), and other listed infrastructure companies.

Take the example of Realty Income (O). The REIT is today offering a 5.4% dividend yield, and it has managed to grow it for 30 years in a row. Not even the dotcom crash, Great Financial Crisis, or the pandemic could stop its dividend growth track record:

It is so defensive because it owns service-oriented, necessity net lease properties such as grocery stores, quick service restaurants, and convenience stores, all leased on a long-term basis to tenants that pay all property expenses, and rents are automatically increased each year according to contractual rent escalations.

Today, Realty Income’s dividend is arguably the safest it has ever been, given that the REIT has low leverage, an A- credit rating, the biggest and best diversified portfolio ever, and a historically low dividend payout ratio of 70%, which allows the REIT to keep reinvesting in additional properties to grow its cash flow.

The REIT has already hiked its dividend a few times this year, and the steady dividend hikes are expected to continue for many years to come as the REIT just keeps replicating its highly successful model.

Is Realty Income riskier than a bond investment?

I would say that it depends on how you perceive risk. If risk to you is synonymous with volatility, then yes, it is without a doubt a lot riskier than bonds.

But if you see risk as the failure to meet long-term income needs due to the gradual loss in purchasing power of your income, then I would argue that Realty Income is a lot safer than bonds.

The REIT pays a 5.4% dividend yield, but it is also steadily growing its cash flow and dividend over time, which protects you against inflation. In fact, ever since the company went public in 1994, it has earned investors a 14% average annual total return, beating even the S&P 500 (SPY):

I would add that Realty Income is by no means an exception. There are plenty of similar opportunities that allow you to build a well-diversified portfolio of high-yielding companies with defensive business models that have consistently grown their dividend over time, even through various crises.

Take the example of Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). It is today offering a near 7% dividend yield and has grown its dividend for 27 years in a row, and generated 13% average annual total returns for its shareholders.

The business itself is very defensive as it owns essential energy infrastructure that our society desperately needs, and it earns steady cash flow from long-term contracts. Just like Realty Income, it also uses little leverage, has an A- credit rating, and has a historically low dividend payout ratio.

So here you need to ask yourself:

Would you rather earn about 5% yield from Treasuries or bonds, which, after taxes and inflation, would leave you with close to nothing, meaning that if you spend the money, your capital will lose purchasing power over time.

Or would you prefer to invest in defensive inflation-protected equities that pay significant and growing income over time?

The yield itself might be comparable, but bonds offer stability while equities offer inflation protection.

I view inflation as a much bigger long-term risk than volatility, and therefore, I don’t really see a point in investing in bonds, even for retirees. The exception would be if you know that you don’t deal well with volatility, but that’s also a skill that can be learned.

data.title: FactSet Is Still Struggling, But Already A Bit Undervalued
data.description:
FactSet Research Systems Inc. (NYSE:FDS) is a company I covered from time to time in the last few years, and I was always rather bearish about the stock or neutral at best. In my opinion, FactSet was not a good investment, and this is including my last article, which was published in December 2024. In the last article, I wrote that the combination of growth slowing down and the stock price increasing is challenging. I wrote in the conclusion of my last article:

As a result, we can only see FactSet overvalued at this point. The slight overvaluation is certainly not a reason to sell the stock, but in my opinion, it is also not a “Buy” – unless the company can demonstrate it is capable to grow at a faster pace as it has in the last few years.

And the next chance for the company to demonstrate its ability to grow at a higher pace is next week. On December 19, 2024, FactSet will report first quarter results for fiscal 2025 and currently revenue is expected to be $565 million (resulting in about 4% growth) and GAAP earnings per share are estimated to be $3.93 (resulting in only about 2.5% growth).

Since my last article was published about nine months ago, the picture has changed. The main reason we must question now if the rating is still accurate was caused by the stock price decline. While the S&P 500 increased 9%, FactSet declined 31%, and after such a massive move in the stock price, we might see FactSet as undervalued now. However, the growth rates are still lowered compared to past results—and this is including the recently reported fourth quarter results for fiscal 2025 and the guidance for fiscal 2026. In the following article, we look at the company and stock again to determine if FactSet is a ”buy” now.

Results: Solid, But Not Great

On September 18, 2025, FactSet reported fourth-quarter results for fiscal 2025, and while the company missed estimates for earnings per share, it did beat slightly on revenue expectations. Year-over-year, revenue increased from $562.2 million in Q4/24 to $596.6 million in Q4/25 – resulting in 6.1% year-over-year growth. Operating income increased 38.6% year-over-year from $127.9 million in the same quarter last year to $177.3 million in this quarter. Diluted earnings per share increased from $2.32 in Q4/24 to $4.03 in Q4/25 – resulting in 73.7% year-over-year growth for the bottom line.

The high growth was mostly driven by lower operating expenses—especially for selling, general, and administrative. However, when looking at adjusted earnings per share, FactSet reported an 8.3% year-over-year growth from $3.74 in the same quarter last year to $4.05 in this quarter.

For fiscal 2025, FactSet also reported 5.4% year-over-year growth from $2,203.1 million in fiscal 2024 to $2,321.7 million in fiscal 2025. Adjusted diluted earnings per share increased 3.2% year-over-year from $16.45 in the previous year to $16.98 in this year, while GAAP earnings per share grew 11.8% year-over-year from $13.91 in the previous year to $15.55 in this year.

When looking at ASV (annual subscription value), we see EMEA struggling a little bit, and ASV grew only 4% year-over-year to an ASV of $586 million. The Americas region increased its ASV by 6% to $1,542 million. The highest ASV growth was reported by the Asia-Pacific region, which grew 7% year-over-year to $243 million.

And while Institutional Buy-Side clients are still responsible for the biggest part of the annual subscription value, growth stemmed especially from “Wealth,” which increased its organic ASV 10% year-over-year.

Growth: Slowing Down?

When looking at the bigger picture, we see growth slowing down over the last three decades as well as over the last few quarters. However, we also see slight improvements in the recent past with growth rates accelerating again – at least a little bit – although 6% revenue growth seems to be disappointing when considering growth rates of 20% to 30% in the early 2000s.

For fiscal 2026, revenue is expected to be in the range of $2,423 million to $2,448 million—resulting in around 5% growth for the top line. Organic ASV is also expected to grow in the mid-single digits (between $100 million and $150 million).

And while GAAP diluted earnings per share are expected to be in a range of $14.55 to $15.25 (resulting in a 10% to 14% decline), adjusted earnings per share are expected to be in a range of $16.90 to $17.60 (resulting in 8.5% to 13% bottom-line growth).

When looking at the expectations, FactSet is certainly continuing to grow at a solid pace. Mid-single-digit growth for the top line and double-digit growth for adjusted earnings per share are certainly solid numbers. Our problem is that we are comparing current growth rates to previous growth rates and are rather disappointed by the relatively low growth rates FactSet is reporting right now.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

However, when looking at valuation multiples and determining an intrinsic value, we can see that high-growth rates are not necessary at this point to make FactSet interesting for investors. At this point in time, FactSet is certainly not a bargain, but double-digit growth is also not necessary for the stock to be fairly valued.

This is already obvious when looking at the valuation multiples the stock is trading for. At the time of writing, FactSet is trading for 23 times earnings and 21.5 times free cash flow. And although FactSet has been trading for lower valuation multiples in the last ten years, this is clearly below the 10-year average and a more reasonable valuation multiple for FactSet.

And when using a discounted cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value, it also seems like FactSet Research might be fairly valued or even a bit undervalued at this point. As always, we are calculating with the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (38.16 million at the end of Q4/25) and a discount rate of 10%, as this is the annual return we like to achieve at least. The basis for our calculation could be the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $577 million, and a reasonable assumption.

In the last four quarters, FactSet reported a free cash flow margin of 25.2%, which is almost in line with the long-term average of 24.3% (and certainly in line with the average of the last ten years). And let’s be cautious at this point and assume FactSet will only be able to grow at a similar pace as in the last few quarters, and we assume 6% growth for the next ten years. Additionally, we will also be optimistic and assume FactSet can grow 6% till perpetuity due to the wide economic moat (we will get to this). When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $378.01, and even when being a little more pessimistic and assuming only 4% growth till perpetuity, we get an intrinsic value of $278.73. And even in this very pessimistic scenario, the stock is close to its intrinsic value (the stock is trading around $300 at the time of writing).

Reasons For More Optimism

We can also make the case for more optimism when talking about FactSet at this point, as the company has several ways to grow its bottom line. Aside from improving its annual subscription value (for example, by price hikes), the company can also focus on improving its margins. Both growth opportunities—higher revenue and higher margins—can be driven by AI in the foreseeable future. During the last earnings call, the management team pointed towards progress on the AI roadmap and the accelerating AI investments, which might lead to lower personnel expenditures in the years to come.

Another contributor to growth for FactSet Research is share buybacks. During the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025, the company repurchased about 260k shares for $106.6 million under the company’s previous share repurchase program. Starting September 1, 2025, a new share repurchase program can be used, which was approved by the Board of Directors in June 2025 and has a volume of up to $400 million.

Share buybacks have been a capital allocation tool for FactSet in the last 20 years, and during that timeframe the company repurchased about 1.5% of shares annually.

In total, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 8.98% between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2029. And in the past, FactSet grew its earnings per share at least in the high single digits and in many cases even in the double digits. Therefore, assuming 9% growth for the next ten years followed by 6% growth till perpetuity also seems like a reasonable assumption and one we can justify. This calculation leads to an intrinsic value of $467.50 for FactSet.

Wide Economic Moat

I have argued we can calculate with 6% growth till perpetuity, which is a lot higher than the terminal growth rate of 2% to 4%, which is recommended by the CFI, but I did not provide a reason for a higher growth rate. The answer, however, is simple: FactSet Research has a wide economic moat around its business, justifying higher growth rates in perpetuity.

For starters, FactSet Research clearly outperformed the S&P 500 over the long run. Since the mid-1990s, the S&P 500 increased about 900%, while FactSet Research increased about 7,000% (stock is volatile right now). However, in the last ten years, FactSet did clearly underperform the S&P 500: while the index increased 231% in the last ten years, FactSet’s stock price increased only about 80%. Similar to the stock price, we also see the company’s return on invested capital metrics declining in the recent past. 10 years ago, the company reported a return on invested capital of 30%, 40%, or even 50%, and in the last 10 years, the return on invested capital was 26.68% on average.

However, in the last five years, FactSet still reported an average return on invested capital of 18.81%, which is still a very good metric. And in combination with the rather stable gross margin and operating margin (in the last few years both metrics improved again), we have strong hints for a wide economic moat around the business.

FactSet Research is also mentioning proudly in its investor relations section that the company reported 45 consecutive years of revenue growth and 27 consecutive years of adjusted earnings growth as a public company, which is also underlining the high quality of the business. Additionally, the company is reporting an ASV retention above 95% and a client retention of 91% - both metrics indicate a sticky business.

And in my opinion, the wide economic moat FactSet clearly has, will lead to a stable business for FactSet and might be a strong argument for higher growth rates in the years to come. Even when these businesses are sometimes struggling, they often manage – due to the wide economic moat – to perform well, generate huge amounts of free cash flow year after year, and are therefore usually great investments.

Conclusion

In my opinion, FactSet is a great business with a wide economic moat and sustainable business model. In the recent past, the business was struggling, and therefore a lower stock price was probably justified. However, FactSet is now down about 40% from its previous all-time high, and this decline makes it rather undervalued and a “buy” at this point.

But while FactSet is trading below its intrinsic value in my opinion, we still should be cautious. Stock markets usually tend to swing from one extreme sentiment to another. It is rather the exception that stocks are trading close to the intrinsic value – instead, the stock price usually swings from overvaluation to undervaluation and is kind of oscillating around its intrinsic value. In case of FactSet this could lead to even lower stock price over the next few quarters—although the stock seems oversold right now and the previous low around $295 could be a strong support level.

data.title: Disney: Patience Is Needed (Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in March of this year, I initiated The Walt Disney Company (NYSE:DIS) at a sell rating. While I believed that the fundamental setup was more or less neutral, there were extraordinarily bearish technicals and that led to my expectations of further stock declines. After the publishing of that article, the stock did decline but in May, Disney quickly rebounded.

In the below analysis, I'll show that Q3 results were respectable, but there are bottom-line pressures expected in the coming quarters. That said, the company's long-term outlook looks positive as strategic moves like Star India make the company well positioned. With a balance of near-term challenges but longer-term opportunity, the valuation seems fair, and thus I upgrade the stock to a hold.

Streaming User Metrics

We'll start off today's discussion with a look at their user metrics as it will give us a good idea of the demand for their all-important streaming business. The company reported FY2025 Q3 results back in the first half of August, and overall I would say that subscription figures are a bit sluggish. In total, Disney+ saw just a 1% increase in paid subscribers, with International leading the way for growth. This growth rate is unchanged from the previous quarter, but it should be noted that their Domestic market saw no growth in Q3. That may be a sign of consumer cautiousness in North America. As for average revenue per paid subscriber, as a whole Disney+ again saw 1% of growth. However, this time, it is a notable slowdown from Q2's 3% growth. This may indicate that subscribers are opting for their less expensive streaming services. Overall, I would say Disney+, a key growth driver, is seeing uninspiring results.

As for Hulu, results were similarly sluggish. Their number of paid subscribers increased by 1%. That represents a slowdown from an already low growth rate of 2% seen in Q2. For average monthly revenue per paid subscriber, both SVOD Only and Live TV + SVOD saw basically no growth YoY. SVOD Only actually steadied after a 1% decline in Q2 but Live TV + SVOD slowed from 1% of growth in Q2. As a whole, Hulu results are also unexciting and so overall Disney's streaming services don't seem to be driving significant growth for the company currently. Moving forward, investors should definitely keep an eye on these key metrics as at the present the trajectory of their streaming business is disappointing.

Revenues Disappoint

For top-line results, Disney reported revenues of $23.7 billion, up 2% YoY. This differs slightly from the figure shown in the above chart, but nonetheless, you get the idea of how their top line performed. As you can see, growth slowed considerably from the previous quarter's 7% increase. This is obviously unfavorable, and it also missed consensus expectations by a potentially worrying amount of $103.23 million. Their sports segment was a drag on performance as there was a 5% decline in revenues, but their eliminations also increased YoY. On the other hand, their experiences segment led the way with 8% growth to $9.086 billion in revenues. Experiences saw acceleration, and honestly that is a pleasant surprise for me. With consumer confidence being shaky in the U.S. and other international markets, lower attendance at Disney theme parks would have been intuitive.

Star India

Before moving into a discussion about their profitability and cash flows, I want to briefly mention their Star India business. Back in late 2024, Disney and Reliance Industries completed the formation of a joint venture. This combines Disney's entertainment services with some media and entertainment businesses that are controlled by RIL. Disney holds a 37% interest in this joint venture. Firstly, India is one of the largest markets for streaming services worldwide, so Disney's reshaping of operations in this market is a highly strategic one in my view. The integration with a domestic company will likely allow for bundling of entertainment services, potentially leading to higher use of their streaming services over time. With the market being a high potential one and Disney partnering with a strong player in this market, I believe they have a recipe for growth moving forward.

EPS, Cash Flow & Returns

Despite the sluggishness in top line growth, Disney's bottom line saw healthy growth as a reflection of strong profitability. They reported an adjusted EPS of $1.61, up 16% YoY. While the top line missed expectations by a potentially worrying margin, EPS beat estimates by $0.17, so profitability was indeed surprisingly strong for the company. However, as you can see above, cash generation was even more impressive. Disney generated a total of $3.669 billion in operating cash flows, representing a 41.01% increase from the year ago quarter. Even though that is a slowdown of growth from Q2, this large increase far outpaces both top- and bottom-line growth. The company did increase their CAPEX by 30.40% to $1.78 billion as they continue to invest in the business, but overall FCFs are also strong at $1.889 billion. However, the company's capital distributions to shareholders may be a bit disappointing.

Firstly, Disney did not pay a dividend for their Q3 in 2025 nor 2024. As you can see on the right, the company's overall annual dividend isn't rated too favorably by Seeking Alpha. With a yield of just 0.87% and a inconsistent record of dividend growth in recent times, it should be no surprise that the ratings for yield and consistency are at D and F, respectively. Safety is also correctly awarded an A as cash flows are strong, but the growth rating may be too generous. Disney's dividend has a five-year growth rate of -3.80%, so I would say overall the company's dividend is not an attractive one. In terms of share repurchases, there is a turn for the worse. Back in FY2024 Q3, Disney bought back $1.522 billion in stock. In this year's Q3, they only bought back $711 million. That's less than half, so the company may be a bit cautious, or they may need extra cash to invest later. Nevertheless, investors seeking capital returns may see that as a deterrent.

EPS Outlook

Disney raised their guidance for the bottom line after a strong Q3. In their Q2 earnings report, the company stated that they expected an annual adjusted EPS of $5.75, representing growth of 16%. In their most recent earnings report for Q3, they upped their expectations to $5.85, which would be an 18% increase over FY2024 levels. That is a respectable amount of growth, but do note that it does represent a slowdown from the previous year's 32% increase to $4.97 from FY2023 levels.

Furthermore, the above chart shows the consensus expectations for EPS for the next four quarters, and despite the raise of annual guidance, Q4 is actually expected to be a negative growth quarter. Even moving beyond that, projected growth rates for the first three quarters of FY2026 seem to be a bit disappointing. Personally, I find these expectations to be unsurprising as the sluggishness in streaming subscription metrics may be catching up to the company. Disney's reliance on business efficiency improvements won't be enough to keep bottom-line growth up. As stated earlier, their India joint venture is a potential growth driver down the road, but for the near term, sluggishness should be expected.

Valuation Is Appropriate

Currently, Disney trades at a forward P/E of 19.82. That is quite elevated for this year, but when taking a multiyear view, the multiple is only at around average levels. I believe there are reasons to be a bit cautious at this valuation. Firstly, for Q3, Disney's experiences segment came through to power growth with subscription metrics for streaming being sluggish. Furthermore, profitability was strong as EPS growth outpaced revenue growth significantly. Therefore, overall, Q3 was a respectable quarter. Moving forward, in the near term, there are some concerns about bottom-line growth, but I believe the company is positioning itself well for the long term. The reorganization of their India efforts along with other strategic decisions like partnering with Webtoon Entertainment (WBTN) and signing a streaming deal with Atresmedia show that they are committed to long term growth. They are moving into different types of media and different geographic locations and that should expand their market moving forward. Therefore, I believe despite some near term challenges ahead, the mildly high forward P/E ratio is justified with the company well positioned to continue being an entertainment and media leader.

Conclusion

Keeping this conclusion clear and concise, I would say that Disney is a solid company, but it may not be a buy yet. While weak consumer confidence isn't affecting their experiences segment too much, streaming metrics are a bit worrying. In addition, despite strong profitability in Q3, moving forward, EPS growth will likely be mixed. Therefore, the near term won't be smooth sailing for the company. That said, initiatives like the reorganization of their India efforts and other new partnerships and deals show that the company is actively seeking growth drivers, and over time that should be beneficial to shareholders. This is also the reason that I see the valuation as justified, so I have decided to upgrade Disney to a hold on its long term prospects.

data.title: PMTU Vs. IBHH: Evaluating Single Note To HY Bond ETF
data.description:
Introduction

One decision fixed income investors face is the risk level they are willing to accept for the potential Yield-to-Maturity, or YTM, they would earn if the asset is held until maturity. Last summer I compared PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust NT 8.5% 28 (NYSE:PMTU) against similar Notes from other mREIT firms where I rated it a Hold as some of the other Notes seemed better choices (article link). This time I changed the risk profile used by comparing the Note against the iShares iBonds 2028 Term High Yield and Income ETF (BATS:IBHH): one Note versus a set that all mature the same year as PMTU. I am rating PMTU a Hold until after its pending Call date. As for IBHH, it is a Buy as part of a HY bond laddering strategy.

Both assets had little initial reaction to the Federal Reserve cut to its benchmark interest rate at their September meeting. They signaled more cuts are likely this year. The FOMC opted for a 25 bps cut this time around though some voted for a 50 bps reduction. Keep in mind cuts to the Federal Funds Rate mostly effects the short end of the interest curve, not so much the middle where these assets reside.

PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust review

Unlike the ETF, for the Note the default risk must be evaluated by examining the issuer. Seeking Alpha describes this mREIT as (edited):

PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust, through its subsidiary, primarily invests in residential mortgage-related assets in the United States. The company operates through: Credit Sensitive Strategies, Interest Rate Sensitive Strategies, Correspondent Production segments. The Credit Sensitive Strategies segment invests in credit risk transfer (CRT) agreements and subordinate mortgage-backed securities (MBS). The company primarily sells its loans to government-sponsored entities. The company qualifies as a real estate investment trust for federal income tax purposes. It generally would not be subject to federal corporate income taxes if it distributes at least 90% of its taxable income to its shareholders. The company was incorporated in 2009 and is headquartered in Westlake Village, California.

PennyMac provides this description about themselves:

As a real estate investment trust (REIT), our objective is to provide attractive risk-adjusted returns to our shareholders over the long-term, primarily through dividends and secondarily through capital appreciation. Our investment focus is on mortgage-related assets that we create through our industry-leading correspondent production activities, including mortgage servicing rights (MSRs). In correspondent production, we acquire, pool and securitize or sell newly originated prime credit quality loans.

Source: pmt.pennymac

While profitable, PMT has trouble covering their common stock dividend. That said, interest on the Notes is paid before the EPS calculations but better earnings would provide comfort to Noteholders in my view.

PMT has held steady in the value listed as Total Common Equity, which shows that the three Notes have a 4.4X coverage ratio: decent but not strong.

The quarterly distribution is $.53125.

PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust NT 8.5% 28 review

PMTU is one of three Notes PMT has issued and the first one to be both Callable and matured. None of the major rating agencies rendered an opinion on this Note.

iShares iBonds 2028 Term High Yield and Income ETF review

Seeking Alpha describes this ETF as (edited):

iShares iBonds 2028 Term High Yield and Income ETF is managed by BlackRock Fund Advisors. It invests in fixed income markets of the United States. The fund primarily invests in U.S. dollar-denominated, taxable, fixed-rate, high yield corporate bonds that are rated equal to or below Ba1, BB+, BB+ and above CC by Moody's, S&P and Fitch respectively and BBB or equivalently rated corporate bonds, scheduled to mature between January 1, 2028 and December 15, 2028. The fund seeks to track the performance of the Bloomberg U.S. Universal Index and the Bloomberg 2028 Term High Yield and Income Index. The IBHH ETF was formed on March 08, 2022 and is domiciled in the United States.

Holdings review

iShares provides useful data points on the ETF's portfolio.

Starting with sectors, we see the two main consumer sectors account for over 32%, with Communications (14%), which also has a high consumer exposure, sitting between the two.

The credit rating allocations will scare some investors which is understandable given the 12% exposure to high risk CCC-rated bonds. Overall, Morningstar gives the portfolio an average rating of B+.

Using data provided, I was able to divide the 2028 maturities into each month. After June, matured bonds are invested in 3-month T-bills, not bonds that mature later in the year.

Top holdings

Not counting the cash, the rest of the Top 20 holdings comes to 18% of the portfolio. About 40% of the portfolio have a weight below .25%, comprising 22% of the portfolio weight.

Distributions review

As a reflection of the low portfolio turnover rate (13%), the monthly distribution hasn't varied as much as the change in interest rates might seem to warrant. As the ETF enters its last six months, like other ETFs in this series I expect their distributions to slowly drop as matured bonds are no longer reinvested in other HY bonds, but lower yielding 3-month T-bills.

Comparing the choices

Factor PMTU IBHH
Yield 8.50% 6.58%
YTC NEG NA
YTM 7.96% 6.03%
Fees NA 35bps + trading costs
Risk diversify None 294 bonds held
Default damage possibly severe should be minor
Trading volume 4k daily 91k daily

With PMTU coming Callable on 9/30/25, its above Par price results in a negative YTC so buyers beware. My calculator was not sensitive enough to take into effect there is a interest payment due at the end of the month but PMTU is past the record date for that payment.

When looking at the yield and YTM, PMTU is the better choice but as stated, that comes with Call risk. While maybe small, PMTU still has a higher probability of suffering a big price hit if it defaults versus IBHH having some of its CCC-rated bonds failing. The other limiting factor is its low volume will make it harder to build or sell a large position.

My thought is holding off buying PMTU until after its Call date though I would think PMT would have already announced that if it was going to occur right off, thus my Hold rating for PMTU. As for IBHH, investors okay with owning HY bonds, I rate it a Buy as part of a bond ladder strategy.

Portfolio strategy

This is a classic case where owning an individual Note provides a potentially higher return but that 100% exposure to one Note versus versus a basket of similarly rated bonds brings with greater odds of up to a complete loss of the investment. Unlike the safer ETF, the Note has no carrying cost as the ETF does by charging the investor fees and the cost of the trading activity of the ETF’s portfolio.

Of course the investor can eliminate the single bond risk by owning multiple Notes/bonds across different industries, maturities, and credit ratings. If I remember right, studies have shown a portfolio of 10-20 Notes/bonds provides a good compromise between risk and return if properly diversified.

Another advantage a single Note/bond has over the basket owned by the ETF’s portfolio is the YTC/YTM is known versus what the ETF estimates theirs might be. Of course while a holding might be Called, the ETF won’t be as could happen to individual Notes/bonds.

data.title: Closing Gaps, Opening Opportunities: How Dick's Can Make The Foot Locker Acquisition Pay Off
data.description:
In May Dicks Sporting Goods (NYSE:DKS) and Foot Locker (FL) announced that the former will acquire the latter for approximately $2.5 billion. Many shareholders of DKS were against the idea and initially the shares sold off significantly (approximately 15%). Since then, the shares have recovered and are now trading slightly higher than the moment of the announcement. As the transaction recently closed, I will look into ways DKS can make this transaction work.

The transaction

On May 15th DKS announced the acquisition of Foot Locker for a total of $2.5 billion and gave shareholders of FL a choice between cash and shares. In the end choosing shares was the more valuable option, and the majority of the shareholders of FL opted for this (85.8%, but no fractional shares were given). This led to approximately 9.6 million new shares and a diluted shares outstanding of approximately 90.6 million. The increase in share count will have a dilutive effect as Dick's stand alone NI margin was 8.5% and FL's was 0.2% in 2024. As such it is understandable that many DKS investors weren't happy with the acquisition. Nevertheless, there are ways DKS can still make this work.

Store closures

One of the possibilities that DKS can make this acquisition work is to close some stores. To make an estimation of how attractive this option is, I looked at the proximity of DKS stores and FL stores. To do this I ran a Python code that looped through Open Street Map and found all DKS stores that were within 10 miles of Footlockers. Do note that this excludes the sub brands such as Golf Galaxy and WSS. Although Open Street Map is not exhaustive, it provides a good indication of how many stores overlap. Additionally, while 10 miles is not a great distance, it might be too far for stores that are for example in Manhattan. Although one can argue that Dick's stores tend to be located on the outskirts of cities due to their large footprint and, as such, 10 miles provides a good indication for whether stores overlap or not.

After running the code, I found that 279 stores overlap. This excludes stores where more than 1 overlap (Footlocker tends to have a Footlocker and Footlocker Kids near each other). It also includes a Dick's that is supposed to open in 2026. The majority of these stores are located in California (24 stores), Florida (21 stores), and Texas (20 stores). It also shows that there is a significant overlap between stores (almost 1/3rd of Dick's stores are within 10 miles of a Footlocker)

The fact that these stores overlap does not mean that they will actually be closed. There are multiple reasons to keep certain stores open, such as different customer demographics (which can be seen from loyalty cards for example), both stores are highly profitable or there are other competitors that might take market share if you decide to leave a certain mall as people don't want to or are unable to drive to the store that is slightly further away. Nevertheless, it could improve the profitability of the FL brand, which hasn't been great on its own in the past few years.

Negotiation position

Another positive of the acquisition is that it gives the company a better negotiation position with its key vendors. This will allow the combined company to negotiate better margins, more innovation and other benefits that it would not have been able to as two standalone companies.

This could have a significant positive effect on the acquired FL business, as one of the reasons that Footlocker was struggling over the past few years is that Nike started to focus more on its D2C business. Even though Nike has started to shift back towards wholesalers such as FL and DKS due to their own struggles, FL hasn't recovered yet.

Another issue, according to FL, is that many apparel companies are lacking innovation. As the combined company will be a significant player, it can, to a certain extent, force its suppliers to come up with something new. While in practice, this power might be limited, it does help if your largest customer says it.

On the conference call CEO Ed Stack also mentioned the following:

Well, we think this makes us much bigger and much more important partners to those brands. From what they're going to do from a distribution strategy, one never knows. But everything that we've talked about, everything that we've seen is that they're not looking to change their distribution strategy and broaden it significantly where access to some of this High Heat product would go other places that it doesn't have today.

So from that point of view I am confident that this will make the combined company have the weight to demand certain changes.

Risks

Integration risks

The first key risk that I see with the acquisition is the integration risk. Footlocker is approximately half the size of DKS, meaning that it is a considerable acquisition. It also has a significantly larger store count than Dick's and runs an almost completely different strategy (large stores vs small stores, and 25% vs 59% of merchandise purchases). Furthermore, Michael Lasser (from UBS) mentioned on the conference call that there are multiple cases in retail where a good performing company acquired an underperforming company in which there were complications with creating shareholder value.

Furthermore, there is the fact that FL's international business has been underperforming over the past few years. In Q1 for the international segment (meaning Europe and Asia Pacific) they saw a reduction in comparable sales of 8.5% and in Q2 the performance was even worse at a decline of 10.3%. The question is whether Dick's management will be able to turn this around. While they have plenty of experience in the US market, they were not active in international markets. As someone from outside of the US but who has visited the US multiple times, I experienced first hand that there are clear differences in culture and shopping experience between the two continents. Therefore, I am unsure whether the company will be able to turn this part of the business around.

Leases

One of the risks regarding my thesis is that DKS will be unable to get out of some of the leases. Many leases include a minimum lease term and to get out of the lease the lessee might need to pay a hefty fine. For that reason closing stores might take multiple years. This means that the company will essentially be competing with itself, incurring double cost and thus impacting the margin of the company. To be fair, the positive side of this is that the company can see whether or not the stores can coexist and both be reasonably profitable before pulling the plug. Nevertheless, having multiple underperforming stores will have a negative effect on the margins.

Conclusion

Dick's Sporting Goods recently closed the acquisition of Foot Locker. This gives the company a large market share in the Sports Apparel category. Nevertheless, many people are skeptical whether the acquisition will work. In this article I provided two ways the company can make it work. One of these is to close stores that are in close proximity of each other and potentially cannibalizing sales. While the other is using the combined size of the companies to demand certain benefits from their suppliers, which can include more innovation and higher margins.

Unfortunately, this does not mean that there are no risks to the thesis. The main one is integrating Foot Locker's business into DKS business. From that perspective, previous retail deals do not provide much hope as a successful retailer acquiring an underperforming one hasn't led to much shareholder value creation. The second risk is the fact that closing stores might take time or cost a decent amount of money as they need to get rid of their lease.

Despite the risks I remain positive and currently think that the positives of the deal outweigh the negatives. Especially given the significant overlap of stores, I think that the company can make great strides there.
